label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. I need to figure out the minimum number of socks I have to pull out from a drawer in the dark to guarantee at least two matching pairs. The drawer has 12 red, 8 blue, and 6 green socks. Hmm, so there are three colors. Let me think about the worst-case scenario because the question is about guaranteeing two pairs, right? First, I remember that for these kinds of problems, the pigeonhole principle is usually involved. For example, if you want to make sure you have at least one pair, you consider the worst case where you pick as many as possible without getting a pair. But here, it's two pairs. So maybe I need to consider the worst case where I get as many socks as possible without having two pairs, and then add one more sock to ensure that I get the second pair. Wait, but what counts as a pair? A pair is two socks of the same color. So two pairs would be two sets of two socks each of the same color. They could be of the same color or different colors. For example, two red and two blue would be two pairs. But I need to make sure that no matter how the socks are drawn, I have at least two pairs. So the worst-case scenario would be where I have as many socks as possible without having two pairs, and then adding one more sock would force me to have two pairs. Let me try to think through this. First, let's consider the maximum number of socks you can have without having two pairs. How can that happen? Well, you could have one pair and then as many single socks as possible without forming another pair. Or maybe even all single socks. But since we need at least two pairs, the worst case would be when you have one pair and then the rest are all single socks of different colors. Wait, but there are three colors. Let me think. Suppose I have one pair (two socks) of one color, and then one sock each of the other two colors. That would be 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 socks, with only one pair.
